items.prompt: An unsorted integer array must answer whether two distinct elements sum to target T. The array may be reordered and only existence is required. Which strategy uses sorting to create a valid directional search?
constraints: Any valid pair of distinct occurrences is acceptable.
Integer addition uses a sufficiently wide type for the input bounds.

options.text: Sort ascending and test only adjacent pairs.
Sort ascending, place one pointer at each end, and move the side that can change the sum toward T.
Keep the unsorted order and use the first and last positions as monotone boundaries.
Sort ascending and move both pointers inward after every comparison, regardless of the sum.

feedback.reason: Numeric sorting makes the pair sum monotone in each pointer, which justifies discarding one boundary after each comparison.
feedback.details: With left < right, a sum below T cannot be repaired by replacing right with a smaller value, so advancing left is safe. A sum above T cannot be repaired by replacing left with a larger value, so decrementing right is safe. The sorted order supplies this proof. Moving both pointers can discard a valid partner, and adjacency alone is not sufficient for an arbitrary target sum.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_move_both: Changing both boundaries can skip a pair that reuses one current endpoint with a different partner.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_scan_adjacent_only: A target-sum pair can be far apart in sorted order and need not have an adjacent witness.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.preserve_order_ends: Unsorted boundary values provide no monotone relation between pointer movement and the resulting sum.

items.prompt: Order one complete iteration of a sorted two-pointer pair-sum search after left and right have been initialized.
constraints: The loop condition is left < right.

elements.text: Re-evaluate left < right before the next iteration.
Return on equality; otherwise move left for a small sum or right for a large sum.
Compute values[left] + values[right] in a type wide enough for the bounds.

feedback.reason: The movement decision depends on the current wide sum, and the distinct-occurrence boundary must be checked again after movement.
feedback.details: The algorithm maintains the invariant that every unexamined candidate lies within the current closed pointer interval. The current endpoint sum determines which entire boundary row or column is impossible. Movement occurs only after that comparison. Rechecking left < right prevents using one occurrence twice. Computing in a wider type belongs before the comparison because overflow could reverse the apparent ordering relation.

items.prompt: For target 10, which input can legally satisfy a sorted two-pointer search using value 5 twice?
constraints: A pair must use two distinct array positions.
Equal values are allowed when they come from separate occurrences.

options.text: [], because missing values are treated as zero.
[5], because the same position can serve as both pointers.
[5, 5], because left and right reference distinct positions.
[5, 6], because being close to the target is sufficient.

feedback.reason: Equal values form a valid pair only when the pointers identify two distinct occurrences.
feedback.details: The left < right loop condition encodes the distinct-position contract. Two stored 5s can occupy different positions and sum to 10. A singleton cannot be reused unless the problem explicitly permits selecting one occurrence twice, which ordinary pair-sum contracts do not. Duplicate skipping, when added for unique-output variants, must not collapse the only two occurrences before their pair is considered.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_five: Using one index twice violates the distinct-occurrence requirement represented by left < right.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty: The algorithm does not fabricate zero-valued elements or any missing occurrence.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.five_and_six: The required relation is exact equality to 10, not nearest sum.

items.prompt: A test harness requires a sort-based pair search to traverse equal-price records reproducibly. Any valid price pair is acceptable, but records tied on price must have a deterministic order. Which preprocessing rule satisfies that requirement without changing the pointer sum invariant?
constraints: Each record has a unique recordId.
Pointer comparisons use price only after sorting.

options.text: Sort by price but randomly shuffle each equal-price block before searching.
Sort by price only and assume tied records will always appear in the same order.
Sort by recordId and use prices only in the sum comparison.
Sort by (price, recordId), then run the price-based pointer search.

feedback.reason: Record ID completes the tie order while price remains the primary monotone key used by pointer movement.
feedback.details: The pointer proof depends on prices being nondecreasing, so a secondary record ID does not disturb it. It does make the traversal and first encountered identity pair reproducible across implementations and input permutations. A price-only comparator can leave tie order to stability or prior order. Sorting primarily by ID destroys the monotone price sequence, while randomizing ties deliberately violates reproducibility.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.price_only: Comparator equality among distinct records leaves their relative order under-specified for the stated reproducibility contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.id_only: Record-ID order does not make endpoint prices monotone, so the pointer elimination proof fails.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_ties: Random tie order is the opposite of the deterministic traversal requirement.

items.prompt: A sort-then-two-pointer solution must return original array indices. Select all statements that are required for correctness.
constraints: The returned indices must identify two distinct source occurrences.

options.text: If the API requires ascending index order, normalize the two saved original indices before returning.
The current left and right positions in the sorted array are the requested original indices.
After finding values, search the original array by value without tracking which duplicate occurrences were used.
Each sorted occurrence must retain its original index or an equivalent stable identity.

feedback.reason: Sorted pointer positions are search state; the output must be reconstructed from preserved source identities and formatted to its own contract.
feedback.details: Decorated occurrences let pointer movement operate on sorted values while preserving the exact source positions. The output order may have a separate normalization rule, such as lower index first, that is unrelated to left and right in value order. Value-only recovery becomes ambiguous for duplicates and can accidentally return one occurrence twice. Identity must therefore survive from preprocessing through result construction.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_pointer_positions: Left and right index the transformed sequence, not the source array requested by the API.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.recover_by_value_only: Duplicate values make occurrence recovery ambiguous and can violate the distinct-index contract.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.carry_original_index: Without source identity attached to each occurrence, the sort destroys the required index mapping.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.normalize_output_pair: Output formatting is a separate contract and may require reordering the two saved indices.

items.prompt: For one unsorted pair-sum existence query, expected O(n) hash-set lookup is permitted, original order must remain unchanged, and O(n) auxiliary space is acceptable. Which strategy assessment is correct?
constraints: Hash operations have the stated expected-time assumption.
No later query reuses a sorted order.

options.text: A hash-set scan is a reasonable O(n)-expected alternative; sorting is not automatically better.
The use of two values means sorting and two pointers are mandatory.
Without mutating the input, only an O(n^2) pair enumeration is possible.
Sorting is preferable because sort-then-two-pointers is O(n) overall.

feedback.reason: The hash method meets the stated one-query and immutability constraints with a better expected time bound than comparison sorting.
feedback.details: Sort-then-two-pointers is a valid deterministic strategy when reordering or copying and O(n log n) time are acceptable. It is not universally dominant. Here a complement set preserves input order and answers the single query in expected linear time using the allowed memory. The decision could reverse under strict memory limits, adversarial hash requirements, or repeated operations that reuse sorted order.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_is_mandatory: Pair structure does not prescribe one family; hash complement lookup is a complete competing mechanism.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_is_linear: Comparison sorting remains O(n log n), so the total pipeline is not linear under the stated assumptions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic_only: An auxiliary hash set preserves the input and avoids enumerating every pair.

items.prompt: An array may be reordered, and the result needs only pair values rather than source indices. A comparison sort is followed by one opposite-end pointer pass. What is the complete worst-case time complexity?
constraints: The comparison sort has O(n log n) worst-case time.
Each pointer moves inward at most n positions in total.

feedback.reason: The opposite-end pass is linear, but the preceding comparison sort dominates the complete pipeline at O(n log n).
feedback.details: The two pointers move monotonically inward, so the search phase performs O(n) pointer advances. That fact does not erase preprocessing. Comparison sorting costs O(n log n), and O(n log n) + O(n) simplifies to O(n log n). The answer is intentionally time-only: source identity and defensive-copy space are outside this values-only, mutation-permitted contract.

items.prompt: Select all pair objectives that support a direct opposite-end pointer rule after ascending numeric sort under the stated constraints.
constraints: For product objectives, values may include negative, zero, and positive integers.
The pointer rule must be justified without an additional data structure or case partition.

options.text: Determine whether some pair product equals P with unrestricted signs using one uniform endpoint rule.
Determine whether some pair sum equals T.
Count pairs whose sum is at most T using ordered boundary counts.
Determine whether some pair has bitwise XOR equal to T.

feedback.reason: Sorted addition changes monotonically with either endpoint, while XOR and unrestricted-sign multiplication do not support one uniform elimination rule.
feedback.details: For sums, increasing left cannot decrease the sum and decreasing right cannot increase it. That supports exact search and batched counting of all partners under a threshold. XOR changes discontinuously with bit patterns. Products over mixed signs have different monotonic regions and need sign partitioning or another method before a pointer proof can be made. The transfer signal is monotone endpoint influence, not simply a two-value expression.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.xor_equals_target: Numeric order does not make XOR monotone, so moving an endpoint based on the current XOR can discard valid pairs unpredictably.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mixed_sign_product: Negative and positive regions reverse product behaviour; one unpartitioned endpoint rule is not complete.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sum_equals_target: Sorted endpoint sums provide the classic one-boundary-at-a-time elimination proof.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.count_sum_at_most: When an endpoint pair is small enough, sorted order identifies an entire block of qualifying partners.
